subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the fall of the Western Roman Empire, including difficulty governing its large territory, political corruption, economic instability, and attacks by Germanic tribes, and identify the continuation of the Eastern Roman Empire as the Byzantine Empire.

description: Explain the expulsion of the Jews from their homeland by the Romans, which began the Jewish diaspora.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the origins and central features of Christianity: • Key Person(s) Jesus, Paul • Sacred Texts: The Bible • Basic Beliefs: monotheism, sin and forgiveness, eternal life, Jesus as the Messiah

description: Explain the polytheistic religion of ancient Rome, with respect to beliefs about the humanlike qualities of the deities and their importance in everyday life.

description: Analyze how innovations in engineering and architecture contributed to Roman expansion, including the role of: • Aqueducts • Domes • Arches • Roads • Bridges • Sanitation • The Colosseum

description: Describe the characteristics of Julius Caesar’s rule, including: • Leadership in the military • Popularity amongst plebeians • Role as dictator for life • Assassination

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the class system of ancient Rome, including the role of patricians, plebeians, and slaves in Roman society.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the government of the Roman Republic, including: • Branches of government • Representative democracy • Checks and balances • The rule of law and the Twelve Tables • Civic participation

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how the geographical location of ancient Rome contributed to its political and economic growth in the Mediterranean region and beyond.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and locate the geographical features of ancient Rome, including: • Constantinople • Mediterranean Sea • Italian Alps • Rome • Italian Peninsula • Tiber River

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the unification of the Greek city-states by Macedonia, and analyze the impact of Alexander the Great and the diffusion of Hellenistic culture.

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe the purposes of major Greek architecture, including the Parthenon and the Acropolis.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the influence of ancient Greek philosophers (e.g., Aristotle, Plato, and Socrates) and their impact on education and society in Greece.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the historical significance of ancient Greek literature, including how the Iliad and the Odyssey provide insight into the life of the ancient Greeks.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the polytheistic religion of ancient Greece, with respect to beliefs about the humanlike qualities of the deities, their importance in everyday life, and the emergence of the Olympic Games to honor Zeus.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the causes and consequences of the Peloponnesian Wars, including how the growing political conflict between Athens and Sparta led to war and left the city-states open to conquest by the Macedonians.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze the causes and consequences of the Persian Wars, including the role of Athens and its cooperation with Sparta to defend the Greek city-states.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the characteristics of the major Greek city-states of Athens and Sparta, including: • Advantages of each geographic location • Status of women • Approaches to education • Styles of government • Practice of slavery

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the basic concepts of direct democracy and oligarchy.

subject: Social Studies
description: Examine the concept of the polis in Greek city-states, including the ideas of: citizenship, civic participation, and the rule of law.

subject: Social Studies
description: Analyze how the geographical features of ancient Greece, including its mountainous terrain and access to the Mediterranean Sea, contributed to its organization into city-states, role in maritime trade, and colonies in the Mediterranean.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and locate geographical features of ancient Greece, including: • Asia Minor • Mediterranean Sea • Athens • Peloponnesian peninsula • Macedonia • Sparta

subject: Social Studies
description: Describe how the desire for Chinese goods influenced the creation of The Silk Road and initiated cultural diffusion throughout Eurasia, including the introduction of Buddhism into ancient China.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the major accomplishments of the Han Dynasty, including: the magnetic compass, paper making, porcelain, silk, and woodblock printing.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain how the implementation of the philosophy of Confucianism led to the political success and longevity of the Han Dynasty.

subject: Social Studies
description: Explain the significance of the unification of ancient China into the first Chinese empire by Qin Shi Huangdi, beginning the Qin Dynasty.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify the political and cultural problems prevalent in the time of Confucius and how the philosophy of Confucianism and The Analects emphasized the concepts of kinship, order, and hierarchy to address these problems.

description: Describe how the size of ancient China made governing difficult and how the concepts of the mandate of heaven and Legalism emerged as solutions to this problem.

subject: Social Studies
description: Identify and locate geographical features of ancient China, including: • Gobi Desert • Plateau of Tibet • Himalayan Mountains • Yangtze River • Pacific Ocean • Yellow River

subject: Social Studies
description:
Develop geographic awareness by:
  • Using the geographic perspective to analyze relationships, patterns, and diffusion across space at multiple scales (e.g., local, national, global)
  • Analyzing and determining the use of diverse types of maps based on the origin, authority, structure, context, and validity
  • Analyzing locations, conditions, and connections of places and using maps to investigate spatial associations among phenomena
  • Examining how geographers use regions and how perceptions of regions are fluid across time and space
  • Analyzing interaction between humans and the physical environment

subject: Social Studies
description:
Develop historical awareness by:
  • Recognizing how and why historical accounts change over time
  • Perceiving and presenting past events and issues as they might have been experienced by the people of the time, with historical empathy rather than present-mindedness
  • Evaluating how unique circumstances of time and place create context and contribute to action and reaction
  • Identifying patterns of continuity and change over time, making connections to the present

description:
Collect data and information from a variety of primary and secondary sources, including:
  • Printed materials (e.g., literary texts, newspapers, autobiographies, speeches, interviews, letters, personal journals)
  • Graphic representations (e.g., maps, timelines, charts, political cartoons, photographs, artwork)
  • Field observations/landscape analysis
  • Artifacts
  • Media and technology sources
